card.intuition: dp[i][j] = length of LCS of s1[0..i-1] and s2[0..j-1]. If the last characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Otherwise, take the best of skipping one character from either string: max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
card.analogy: Finding the longest shared storyline between two book series: align the books and find the longest sequence of plot points that appear in both, in order, even if other events happen in between.
card.time_complexity: O(m · n) where m, n are string lengths
card.space_complexity: O(m · n) for full table; O(min(m,n)) with rolling array
when_to_use: Diff tools (git diff computes LCS for line-by-line comparison)
Edit distance is closely related: edit_distance = m + n - 2 * LCS(s1, s2)
gotchas: LCS is a subsequence (not substring) — characters need not be contiguous
Backtrack through the DP table to reconstruct the actual subsequence, not just its length
Space-optimised version: only two rows needed since dp[i][j] depends only on dp[i-1][...]

exercise.prompt: Complete the LCS DP table filling logic.
hints: Match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 — the LCS extends by one matching character
No match: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) — skip one character from either string and take the better result
exercise.test_code:
assert lcs('ABCBDAB', 'BDCAB') == 4   # 'BCAB' or 'BDAB'
assert lcs('AGGTAB',  'GXTXAYB') == 4  # 'GTAB'
assert lcs('',  'ABC') == 0
assert lcs('ABC', '') == 0
assert lcs('ABC', 'ABC') == 3
assert lcs('A', 'B') == 0
print('PASS')
